claims.claimText: Immediate life threats are addressed before definitive management of an extremity injury.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Document motor, sensory, pulse, and perfusion findings before and after reduction, splinting, or surgery.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: A single normal neurovascular examination does not replace serial assessment when deterioration remains possible.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: A wound near a fracture should be evaluated for communication with the fracture rather than dismissed by wound size.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Open-fracture antibiotic prophylaxis is a time-sensitive component of initial management.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Traumatic wound care includes assessment of tetanus immunization and prophylaxis needs.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Preserved pulses do not exclude acute compartment syndrome.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Compartment pressure measurement is an adjunct when the examination is unreliable or equivocal and should not delay treatment of a clear clinical syndrome.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Urgent reduction of a gross deformity is followed by repeat neurovascular examination and documentation.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Initial splinting should maintain reduction while accommodating expected swelling and protecting skin.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Post-reduction assessment includes alignment imaging and repeat skin and neurovascular examination.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Definitive fixation timing is governed by both patient physiology and the condition of the soft-tissue envelope.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Damage control orthopaedics uses temporary stabilization when physiologic or soft-tissue conditions make definitive fixation unsafe.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Absolute stability is paired with minimal interfragmentary motion and primary bone healing.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Relative stability permits controlled motion and commonly produces secondary bone healing with callus.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Interfragmentary strain links fracture-gap mechanics to the tissue that can form during healing.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Evaluation of impaired union considers mechanical stability, biology, infection, host factors, and time course.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

claims.claimText: Early postoperative trauma care includes neurovascular surveillance, wound and compartment assessment, mobilization restrictions, and prophylaxis planning.

metadata.sourceNote: Trauma Fundamentals manufacture scope; evidence packet required

decisionPoints.trigger: Hemodynamic instability or unresolved life threat
decisionPoints.action: Prioritize resuscitation and life-saving intervention before definitive fracture fixation

decisionPoints.trigger: Absent pulse, impaired perfusion, expanding hematoma, or evolving ischemia
decisionPoints.action: Reduce gross deformity when appropriate and escalate immediately for vascular evaluation and limb-saving management

decisionPoints.trigger: Fracture with a communicating wound or high suspicion for communication
decisionPoints.action: Cover the wound, document neurovascular status, initiate indicated antibiotics and tetanus assessment, and arrange operative debridement

decisionPoints.trigger: Clinical findings concerning for evolving acute compartment syndrome
decisionPoints.action: Remove constriction, keep the limb appropriately positioned, perform serial examination, and escalate for emergent fasciotomy

decisionPoints.trigger: Unreliable or equivocal examination with ongoing concern for compartment syndrome
decisionPoints.action: Use pressure measurement as an adjunct while maintaining serial assessment and urgent escalation

decisionPoints.trigger: Gross deformity, threatened skin, dislocation, or neurovascular compromise
decisionPoints.action: Perform timely controlled reduction with appropriate analgesia or sedation and document post-reduction status

decisionPoints.trigger: Reduced or unstable acute injury requiring temporary support
decisionPoints.action: Apply a well-padded splint that maintains alignment and accommodates swelling, then repeat examination

decisionPoints.trigger: Physiologic instability, severe soft-tissue injury, or unsafe definitive operative conditions
decisionPoints.action: Choose temporary stabilization and defer definitive fixation until physiology and soft tissues permit

decisionPoints.trigger: Simple reconstructible pattern versus comminuted or biologically vulnerable pattern
decisionPoints.action: Select absolute or relative stability principles appropriate to fracture morphology and biology

decisionPoints.trigger: Failure of expected clinical or radiographic progression toward union
decisionPoints.action: Evaluate mechanical, biologic, infectious, and host contributors before selecting revision treatment

decisionPoints.trigger: New pain escalation, neurologic change, perfusion change, wound concern, or loss of alignment
decisionPoints.action: Repeat focused examination and imaging as indicated and escalate promptly for a limb- or construct-threatening complication
